role_summary: Chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, 1961-1963; co-led the March 1960 Orangeburg student protest.
ai_reading: McDew is the most-centrist voice in the corpus in the strict sense: his position on each of the five concept axes lies between -0.13 and +0.15, the narrowest band the catalog has surfaced so far. The cultural reading of McDew as SNCC's second chairman (between Marion Barry and John Lewis) is bracketing rather than positional; he succeeded an organizer-leader and preceded a strategist-leader, and the embedding catches that bracketing structure as a vector-space centrism. His top semantic neighbors are heterogeneous: Charles McLaurin (entry 17, SNCC Mississippi grassroots), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF lawyer), Harold K. Brown (entry 55), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), and Eddie Holloway (entry 33). No single faction dominates, consistent with the chairman-role centrism but a position the cultural-historical record's SNCC-only label would not have predicted.
biographical_paragraph: In March 1960, on the campus of South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, Charles McDew and Thomas Gaither pulled roughly a thousand students into a coordinated protest against segregation [src: 1]. McDew was a Massillon, Ohio native, born June 23, 1938; he had taken his college years south, and the next year, 1961, he succeeded Marion Barry as the second chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, holding the post until John Lewis took it in 1963 [src: 1]. He was arrested forty-three times in the course of that activism [src: 1]. Fellow organizer Bob Moses described him as "black by birth, a Jew by choice and a revolutionary by necessity"; McDew had converted to Judaism as a high school student, drawn there by the Talmud's writings on justice [src: 1]. Later he taught at Metropolitan State University in Minneapolis, with a focus on civil rights history and African American studies [src: 1]. He died April 3, 2018 in West Newton, Massachusetts, at seventy-nine [src: 1]; his Library of Congress oral history is entry 16 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

sources.title: Charles F. (Chuck) McDew oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
